title: Commit Processing
text: Commit Processing Uniface maintains an internal list of the DBMSs that are currently open. When executing a commit instruction, Uniface goes to each DBMS one by one, issuing a commit instruction. Without Two-Phase Commit Figure 1. Commit Processing Without Two-Phase Commit Send commit to $path1 Send commit to $path2 Send commit to $path3 With Two-Phase Commit Uniface first issues a prepare to commit signal to all the DBMSs which support two-phase commit. If this operation succeeds, Uniface sends a commit to the DBMSs which do not support two-phase commit. The DBMSs from the first round then receive the final commit signal. This process is shown in the example in the figure: Figure 2. Commit Processing with Two-Phase Commit Send commit phase 1 to $path1 Send commit phase 1 to $path3 Send commit to $path2 Send commit phase 2 to $path1 Send commit phase 2 to $path3 Rollback on Error If an error occurs at any point in the process, Uniface will only issue a rollback if it is coded to do so in ProcScript, and only to the DBMSs which have not yet been committed. The possible areas of risk are when one DBMS has received the final commit , and the network fails before the next one can receive its final commit signal. If the network fails at either of these points, an incomplete transaction is committed. Minimized Risks With Two-Phase Commits Every step is taken, however, to make this window of vulnerability small. Uniface first checks the logical integrity of a transaction before doing any modifications. It then checks whether all network connections are functioning before starting to commit. The highest risk is limited to the time between successive connector requests. This is generally a slight fraction of a second. Related Links $TWO_PHASE_COMMIT Enable two-phase commit functionality.
